skill.name: image-outpainting
skill.description: Extend, uncrop, and resize still images with RunComfy outpainting models through the runcomfy CLI.
skill.summary: Routes image outpainting requests to RunComfy edit models for canvas extension and aspect ratio changes.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, URL, filesystem, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review confirmed expected RunComfy CLI execution, third-party network use, and token storage, while the weak-crypto, Ruby backtick, and reconnaissance hits are Markdown or text false positives. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Third-Party CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash(runcomfy *) and instructs agents to run the RunComfy CLI for image outpainting. This is expected functionality, but it executes an external command and depends on the installed CLI, user authentication, and correct handling of user-provided prompts and image URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and example invocations directly show permitted CLI execution. The scope is limited to runcomfy commands, so this is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Image and Prompt Data Sent to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts and image references to RunComfy model endpoints and states that the CLI posts to the Model API. This is necessary for the service, but users should understand that image URLs, prompts, and generated outputs leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes a POST to the RunComfy Model API and includes image URL input examples. This confirms outbound data transfer, but the endpoints match the skill purpose.

low_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user's home configuration directory with file mode 0600. This is normal credential storage, but the hidden file path and token lifetime should be disclosed to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage path and permissions are explicitly documented. Mode 0600 reduces exposure, so the issue is a disclosure and local secret-handling concern rather than evidence of misuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown and Text Scanner False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance alerts were triggered by Markdown code formatting, model names, JSON examples, exit code documentation, or general prose. I found no Ruby execution, cryptographic implementation, or host reconnaissance logic in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation text or Markdown examples, not executable Ruby or cryptographic code. Confidence is high because the only scanned file is SKILL.md and no separate script files exist in the report.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses Bash(runcomfy *) and example runcomfy commands. This pattern is dangerous if command arguments are built from untrusted text without structured quoting, though the skill instructs use of JSON input and limits Bash scope to runcomfy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command execution is directly allowed and documented. The risk is bounded by the command allowlist and explicit shell-injection guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Network Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill links to RunComfy services and describes CLI calls to RunComfy model APIs. This requires network access to third-party infrastructure and may transmit user image data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external service URLs and API behavior are explicit. They align with the advertised function, so this is a known data-flow risk rather than suspicious hidden traffic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Configuration File Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents token storage in a hidden configuration path. Hidden credential files are sensitive and should be protected by permissions, rotation, and user awareness.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden token path is explicitly named. The documented 0600 mode is a mitigating control, so the issue is not high severity.

content.value_statement: Cropped images often need more space for banners, product pages, and social formats. This skill routes outpainting requests to RunComfy edit models and helps preserve the original subject.
actual_capabilities: Extends a still image beyond its original canvas using RunComfy edit endpoints.
Changes square, portrait, or landscape images into new aspect ratios.
Provides model routing guidance for Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, and FLUX Kontext Pro.
Supplies prompt patterns for preserving the original subject and visible area.
Documents CLI invocation, output directory use, and RunComfy troubleshooting references.
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Sends image references and prompts to RunComfy model services.
Does not perform mask-driven local inpainting inside the existing canvas.
Dedicated ComfyUI outpainting workflows are referenced but not invoked through the CLI.

use_cases.title: Create Marketing Banners
use_cases.description: Extend a portrait or square product image into a wide hero image while keeping the product centered.

use_cases.title: Prepare Social Media Formats
use_cases.description: Convert one approved image into vertical, square, or widescreen formats for different channels.

use_cases.title: Recover Cropped Photo Context
use_cases.description: Uncrop a photo by adding plausible background, body, or scene context around the original frame.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Canvas Extension
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple wider image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Outpaint this image to 16:9. Extend the left and right sides with matching background, lighting, and camera distance.

prompt_templates.title: Portrait Uncrop
prompt_templates.scenario: Reveal more of a cropped person while preserving the face.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this portrait downward to show the upper body. Keep the face, pose, clothing style, lighting, and visible background unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Reference-Matched Expansion
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a reference style when the new area must match a specific scene.
prompt_templates.prompt: Outpaint the original image into a wide banner. Match the surrounding style from the reference image and preserve the original subject exactly.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Step Large Outpaint
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for a large expansion with quality checks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the canvas by about 40 percent per pass. After each pass, inspect seams and continue only from the best result.

output_examples.input: A square product photo needs a wide website hero image.
output_examples.output: A 16:9 image with the product preserved and matching space added on both sides.

output_examples.input: A cropped portrait needs more body and background for an editorial layout.
output_examples.output: A taller portrait with added clothing, arms, and background that match the original lighting.

output_examples.input: A landscape image needs a vertical social story version.
output_examples.output: A 9:16 image with extended sky, ground, or environment while retaining the original subject.

best_practices: Use user-provided image URLs only, and confirm that the user has rights to edit them.
State the target aspect ratio and the direction of expansion in the first sentence.
Ask the model to preserve the original subject and visible area exactly.
anti_patterns: Do not outpaint private or regulated images unless the user accepts third-party processing.
Do not rely on vague prompts such as make it bigger without describing the added area.
Do not use one very large expansion when several smaller passes would preserve quality better.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps extend still images beyond their original canvas using RunComfy image editing models.

faq.question: Does it need a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The runcomfy CLI must be installed and authenticated before model runs can succeed.

faq.question: Can it change an image from square to widescreen?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes prompt patterns for square to 16:9 and other aspect ratio changes.

faq.question: Does it edit videos?
faq.answer: No. It is for still images. The skill links to a separate video outpainting resource.

faq.question: Are images processed locally?
faq.answer: No. The CLI sends prompts and image references to RunComfy model services.

faq.question: Can it remove objects inside the current image?
faq.answer: No. That is mask-driven inpainting, which is outside this skill's main workflow.
